story:
He stood outside what used to be his home, old fingers circling the wedding ring on his left hand. The cold cut deep, but not as deep as the ache in his bones. He pictured the plan. He had done it many times. He would not fail now.

He called in the tip, voice dry and cracked: there would be an armed robbery at 23 Infinity just after midnight. Track this phone. Send the police. He dropped the phone on the curb for the responders to recover once they traced the call and moved to the door.

Step one.

He picked the lock and slipped inside. He relocked the door behind him. The gym bag hit the floor with a clank. He didn’t look around. He knew the layout. He had moved through it in too many attempts to count. He crossed the living room, climbed the ten stairs on careful feet, and turned into the first room on the left.

She was there in the moonlight. Young. Radiant. Pregnant. His wife before the years took him and the night took her. He wanted to wake her, to warn her, but he didn’t. He knew it made her die faster. He had tried that before.

He drew a pistol and checked the magazine. Seven rounds. He stared at it a moment like a prayer, then placed it on the bedside table beside a gold ring—the twin of the band still on his own hand.

Step two.

Back downstairs, he opened the bag and laid out three bear traps. One beside the sofa. One in the center of the living room. One at the base of the stairs. The darkness inside the house hid the traps well, as he expected.

Step three.

He took a second gun to the kitchen and crouched in the corner. He played with his ring and waited. He knew what the night would bring.

Step four.

Footsteps scuffed on the porch. The knob rattled, then quiet.

A moment later came movement along the side of the house. He frowned. That was wrong. Then a figure stopped right there in front of the closed kitchen window, his outline visible from the inside. The old man thought about shooting him right there, but remembered that it didn’t work the last time. That’s why he set the traps.

The figure bent, lifted something, and rose with a ladder in both hands. He angled it up against the siding and began to climb toward the second floor. Toward the bedroom window.

It took the old man some time to realize what was happening. Then his stomach dropped: the bedroom window was open a few inches, the latch not fully caught—something he had forgotten to check. But that hadn’t happened before!

He ran.

He cleared the trap by the sofa, jumped over the one at the base of the stairs, hit the stairs two at a time, and turned into the bedroom a heartbeat too late.

She was awake now, startled out of sleep by the sound at the window. Fear clouded her face. The masked man had his gun on her.

Then he saw the old man in the doorway.

Everything froze.

The old man understood at once: if he fired, the intruder would fire first. If he lunged, the same. Slowly, with trembling fingers, he lowered his weapon and set it on the floor. “Don’t hurt her,” he said. His hands were shaking.

She stared at him and whispered, “You wanted to wake me and warn me, but you thought that would make me die faster.”

She moved before either man could. She snatched the pistol from the bedside table and fired. But the shot went wild. The intruder flinched and fired back on instinct. She fell sideways, moonlight flashing off blood on white sheets.

The old man made a sound that did not seem human. He lunged forward. The intruder turned and shot him too. Warmth spilled from his chest. He crumpled.

I failed. But I’ll try again.

Sirens grew faintly somewhere far away as the dark took him.

He woke to the hiss of a pod opening and vomited bile-green on the floor. A headache like lightning. The machine always left him sick. Too much radiation.

When the retching passed, he lay down, pulled a worn notebook close, and wrote:

Attempt #89: Call the police early. Place a gun at her bedside. Set traps. Hide in the kitchen.
Result: Failed.
Action: Close the second-floor window next time.

He stared at the page for a long moment.

In earlier attempts, the burglar had come through the door. Tonight, something had shifted. It didn’t matter why. The result was the same: his wife dead, their unborn child dead.

He had tried ambushing outside. But he was slow. Old age had made him slow; too slow. Twice, the thief had shot him in the head.

He set the notebook aside and held the photo of a younger him and the woman under church arches. He remembered every detail. A petty burglar had taken her and their child. He would keep going back to that night until he brought them home.
